summary: Gruber breaks his usual policy against linking to teaser sites to highlight Push Pop Press, a startup by former Apple designers creating a new kind of digital book. The app uses a physics-based, chrome-free interface where all interaction happens through pinch and swipe gestures, with live video zooming and momentum-based animations. Gruber contrasts this approach with both iBooks (which skeuomorphically mimics paper) and Kindle (which is minimal but joyless), arguing Push Pop represents what Apple's e-book platform should have been. He sees Push Pop's native Cocoa Touch approach as fundamentally more ambitious than HTML-based formats, treating e-books not as digital paper but as software itself.
key_points: Push Pop Press uses a completely chrome-free interface with physics-based gestures for all interaction, making the book feel like an exquisitely crafted game
The books are native Cocoa Touch iOS apps rather than HTML or ePub, delivering smoothness and responsiveness impossible through WebKit
iBooks made the wrong design choice by skeuomorphically mimicking paper books, adding a distracting second layer of chrome between content and reader
Kindle takes a content-focused approach but offers no delight, while Push Pop combines minimalism with genuine wow factor
The fundamental question Push Pop asks is what books can become when you eliminate paper's limitations rather than mimic them
money_quotes: The screen is the book, the book is the screen.
It doesn't just feel like a game — it feels like an exquisitely crafted game.
I find iBooks's design to be distracting. It demos well, but grows tiresome quickly.
In iBooks, there are two layers of chrome: the physical black bezel and metal frame of the device, and the virtual paper book rendered on screen.
No one looks at the Kindle app and says, 'Wow'. But that's exactly what you're going to say when you first see a Push Pop Press book.
Pages look more like they were laid-out by a designer than randomly rendered by a web browser.

What can we do with the idea of a 'book' if we eliminate the limitations of ink and paper, rather than mimic them? E-books that aren't merely rendered by software, but rather e-books that are software.
key_insight: The future of digital books lies not in mimicking paper through skeuomorphism or stripping away delight through minimalism, but in reimagining books as native software that eliminates paper's constraints entirely.
